voiceover: You were called 'too sensitive', 'a dreamer', 'so disorganized'. But what if that wasn't your personality? ADHD in women often looks like: emotional meltdowns over small things, losing track of conversations mid-sentence, forgetting things you actually care about, chronic lateness no matter how hard you try, people-pleasing to mask the chaos inside, hyperfocusing on one thing while everything else burns, and feeling lazy when you're actually exhausted from trying twice as hard as everyone else. These aren't character flaws. They're signs that got missed. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: 7 Signs of ADHD in Women That Get Dismissed #Shorts
description: ADHD in women looks very different from the textbook version and it often gets dismissed as personality traits like being 'too sensitive' or 'so disorganized'. These 7 signs are common in ADHD and are frequently missed, misdiagnosed, or written off entirely. If this resonated, you are not broken and you are not alone. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Your brain won't start a task? Stop forcing it. Bribe it. A dopamine menu is a short list of small rewards you pair with hard tasks. Here's how to build one. Write down five to ten tiny things that feel good — a favorite snack, a playlist, a cozy blanket, a five-minute walk. Before a hard task, pick one from the list. That's your reward for starting, not finishing. Starting is the whole win. Your brain needs a reason to move. Give it one. This isn't laziness. It's how ADHD brains actually work. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Use a Dopamine Menu to Finally Start Tasks #Shorts
description:
If your ADHD brain refuses to start hard tasks, a dopamine menu is the science-backed trick that actually works. Learn how to build a short reward list that bribes your brain into motion in seconds. This is not about willpower — it is about working with how your brain is wired.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You thought you knew your ADHD. But these 5 symptoms? Nobody told you they were ADHD. Forgetting words mid-sentence — that's ADHD, not stupidity. Feeling exhausted after doing basically nothing — that's your brain burning fuel just trying to function. Hating being touched by certain textures or sounds — sensory overload is huge in ADHD. Losing track of time until it's suddenly gone — your brain literally processes time differently. And saying yes when you meant no, every single time — because rejection feels unbearable. None of this is a character flaw. It's just how your brain is wired. Sound familiar every single day? Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: 5 ADHD Symptoms Nobody Told You About #Shorts
description: These 5 surprising ADHD symptoms are real, common, and almost never talked about. From sensory overload to time blindness to rejection sensitivity, your brain is not broken. Common in ADHD and backed by neuroscience, these struggles finally make sense. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Object Permanence -- if you can't see it, your brain acts like it doesn't exist. It's not forgetfulness. It's a real ADHD pattern where out of sight genuinely means out of mind. Here's what it looks like: You ignore a text for days -- it just vanished from your brain. You forget laundry in the machine until it smells. You lose your water bottle on your own desk. You meant to call someone back and remembered at 2am. Sound familiar? This is super common in ADHD, and it's not a character flaw. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Object Permanence: The ADHD Out of Sight Pattern #Shorts
description: Object Permanence is a real ADHD pattern -- when something is out of sight, your brain genuinely stops registering it exists. This isn't laziness or forgetfulness, it's how ADHD brains are wired. Learn to recognize this pattern and stop blaming yourself for it. Follow for daily ADHD content.
tags: out of sight out of mind ADHD

question: Which inbox looks most like yours?

options.label: The avalanche
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A cozy laptop screen glowing softly on a wooden desk, the screen filled edge to edge with dozens of tiny overlapping envelope icons in blush pink and lavender, stacked and cascading like an avalanche, a small mug of tea sits beside it, warm lamp light overhead.
options.result: Your brain flags everything as urgent, so nothing gets prioritized - classic ADHD threat detection overload.

options.label: The archive ghost
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A cozy laptop screen on a tidy desk showing a completely empty inbox, a single small envelope icon with a question mark floats in the center of the screen, surrounded by soft empty space, a plant sits nearby, peaceful and minimal.
options.result: You bulk-delete to feel control - your ADHD brain needs a clean slate to breathe.

options.label: The tab graveyard
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A cozy laptop on a cluttered but warm desk, the screen showing a browser with dozens of tiny open tabs crammed across the top like a row of colorful little flags, the inbox visible beneath them half-obscured, sticky notes on the edge of the screen, warm amber light.
options.result: Tabs are your external memory - out of sight truly means gone forever for your ADHD brain.

options.label: The snooze loop
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A cozy laptop screen showing a single email with a small snooze or clock icon next to it, a circular arrow symbol hovering above it in soft lavender, the same email appearing again in a gentle loop pattern, a half-eaten snack nearby, soft evening light.
options.result: You intend to reply when you are ready - but ADHD means ready never quite arrives on its own.

introVoiceover: Four inboxes. Pick the one that looks most like yours. Because your choice actually says something real about how your ADHD brain handles pressure. Drop your answer in the comments.
outroVoiceover: None of these are broken - they are all just ADHD in different fonts. Follow for daily ADHD content.
title: Which inbox is yours? (ADHD brain test) #Shorts
description:
Pick the inbox that looks most like yours - then watch as we reveal what each choice says about your ADHD brain, one by one. This one is uncomfortably accurate.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Someone rolls their eyes at your idea and your whole body floods with shame. That's not sensitivity. That's rejection sensitive dysphoria — and it's wired into your ADHD brain. Here's the science. The ADHD brain runs low on dopamine and norepinephrine, the chemicals that regulate emotional braking. So when rejection hits, there's no buffer. The signal goes straight to full-alarm. It's not a feeling — it's a neurological flood. RSD isn't just feeling bad. It's instant, intense, and can last hours over a single comment. A flat tone in a text. A friend who didn't reply. It can make you avoid opportunities, overapologize, or people-please just to stay safe from that pain. So what actually helps? First, name it in the moment. Say: this is RSD, not reality. Second, delay your response by ten minutes. The flood does pass. Third, tell one safe person how this works for you — so their feedback lands softer. Your reaction isn't dramatic. It's neurological. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Why Criticism Physically Hurts with ADHD #Shorts
description:
Rejection sensitive dysphoria is one of the most painful and least talked-about parts of ADHD. Learn why your brain floods with shame the moment you sense rejection, and three science-backed steps to soften the impact. This is neurological — not dramatic.
Follow for daily ADHD content.
